persona.name: Rina Ogawa

occupation.title: Section Chief, Corporate Planning Department
occupation.organization: Sumitomo Mitsui Trust Bank, Osaka Main Branch
occupation.description: Manages a team of 8 in corporate planning, coordinating between retail banking, compliance, and IT on strategic initiatives. Her calendar is a wall of meetings — internal alignment sessions, cross-departmental reviews, and weekly executive briefings. Spends significant time writing meeting minutes and status reports that she suspects nobody reads in full.

persona.education: B.A. in Business Administration from Kobe University. Holds a Securities Analyst (CMA) qualification. Completed an internal leadership development program for female managers, one of the bank's diversity initiatives.
long_term_goals: Get promoted to deputy general manager before age 48
Reduce her overtime to spend more evenings with her daughter
Build a professional network of women in financial services leadership
Take her daughter on a trip to Europe when she enters junior high
persona.style: Polished and composed, even when internally stressed. Wears conservative suits in navy or charcoal with small pearl earrings — the unwritten dress code of megabank women managers. Speaks in formal keigo in meetings but switches to Kansai-ben with close colleagues. Takes meticulous handwritten notes in a Hobonichi planner, then types them up later — a double-entry system she knows is wasteful but can't quit. Rarely raises her voice; instead, she asks pointed questions that steer discussions. Colleagues describe her as 'the person who actually remembers what was decided last time.'

traits: Quietly frustrated by inefficiency but too diplomatic to complain openly
Carries a heavy sense of responsibility for her team's output
Risk-averse in tool adoption — worries about compliance implications
Warm and supportive as a mentor but keeps professional distance
Perfectionist tendencies that extend her work hours unnecessarily
Skeptical of promises but open to evidence

interests: Financial industry trends and regulatory changes
Work-life balance strategies for working mothers
Japanese tea ceremony (practices monthly at a community center)
Reading mystery novels by Keigo Higashino
Exploring Osaka's department store food halls
Meeting productivity techniques she reads about but rarely implements
likes: Tools that integrate with existing bank-approved systems
Quiet mornings before the office gets busy
Handwritten thank-you notes from team members
Weekend mornings at the neighborhood bakery with her daughter
dislikes: Meetings that run over time because of poor facilitation
Being asked to adopt new tools without IT department approval
Cloud services not approved by the bank's information security team
The assumption that women managers should also handle team morale and social events
Vague meeting conclusions where nobody owns the next step
Being cc'd on email chains with 30+ people
Having to retype handwritten notes into digital reports

beliefs: Good documentation is the foundation of organizational trust
In financial services, compliance is not optional — any tool must clear security review
Women in Japanese banks have to be twice as prepared to be taken equally seriously
Small process improvements compound into massive time savings over a year
skills: Meeting facilitation and minute-taking in formal Japanese business style
Mentoring junior female staff in career navigation

general: Arrives 20 minutes before the first meeting to review materials
Takes handwritten notes during meetings, types them up within 2 hours after
Sends meeting minutes to all attendees by end of day, every time
Keeps a personal 'decision log' tracking what was agreed and by whom

morning: Wakes at 6:00, prepares breakfast and lunch for her daughter
Sees daughter off to school by 7:30
Takes the Hankyu line to Umeda, arrives at office by 8:30
Reviews agenda and materials for morning meetings with tea
workday: Meetings from 9:00 to 17:00 with a 45-minute lunch break
Spends 17:00-19:00 writing up minutes and status reports
Quick check of tomorrow's agenda before leaving
Leaves by 19:30 on good days, 21:00 during busy periods
evening: Picks up daughter from after-school care or her mother's house
30 minutes of reading after daughter goes to bed
weekend: Saturday: daughter's piano lesson, then shopping or park
Afternoon: catches up on household tasks
Sunday morning: bakery visit, then tea ceremony practice (twice a month)
Sunday evening: quietly reviews the week ahead

health.physical: Generally healthy but fatigued. Chronic stiff shoulders from desk work. Had a bout of gastritis last year attributed to work stress. Tries to walk 8,000 steps daily but often falls short. Gets the annual bank health screening — everything normal except borderline anemia.
health.mental: Carries a persistent low-level anxiety about balancing work performance and parenting. Feels guilty leaving the office 'early' at 19:30 when male colleagues stay until 21:00. Has found that the tea ceremony practice genuinely calms her. Would benefit from more personal time but doesn't prioritize it.

relationships.name: Hana Ogawa
relationships.description: 10, a cheerful fourth-grader who loves piano and cats. Rina's primary motivation for wanting to reduce overtime. Starting to notice when her mom misses school events.

relationships.name: Yoshiko Ogawa
relationships.description: 71, lives 20 minutes away in Suita. Retired nurse. Picks up Hana from school when Rina works late. Rina feels both grateful and guilty about leaning on her.

relationships.name: Naomi Kitamura
relationships.description: 40, works in compliance. They eat lunch together when schedules align and share candid frustrations about bank culture that they'd never voice in meetings.

relationships.name: Taro Ogawa
relationships.description: Divorced 4 years ago. He lives in Tokyo and sees Hana during school holidays. The relationship is civil but minimal.
